canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, "Adaptability" is a broad construct, often used interchangeably with terms like "domain adaptation" and "flexibility," that predominantly refers to a model's capacity to adjust its behavior and performance to new tasks, domains, or data distributions without requiring full retraining. The prevailing usage frames this as aligning a model with specific downstream tasks based on their distributional characteristics or maintaining performance when faced with a domain shift. A smaller subset of work defines adaptability more specifically, for instance as "self-adaptation," where a model modifies its own behavior in real-time without external intervention, or as "adaptivity," which refers to an optimizer's use of parameter-specific update rules. The literature also contains discussion on whether adaptation represents true new learning or, as one paper asks, if it "simply draw[s] out capabilities that the model had already learned" (A Benchmark for Learning to Translate a New Language from One Grammar Book). The construct is operationalized and measured through model performance on a wide array of benchmarks. Papers in this set use instruments such as AndroidWorld and OSWORLD to evaluate adaptability in dynamic agent tasks, while others like Mind2Web, ScreenSpot, AG News, and ChemProt are used to assess performance on new tasks and domains. Adaptability is frequently studied alongside and considered related to Generalization, and some work reports that planning and compositionality can influence a model's adaptive capabilities.

definitions.text: The capacity of a model to acquire new capabilities or knowledge from limited, task-specific reference materials rather than relying on pre-existing training data.
definitions.paper: A Benchmark for Learning to Translate a New Language from One Grammar Book

definitions.text: The ability of a model to adjust its behavior and performance to align with specific downstream tasks based on their distributional characteristics.
definitions.paper: DQ-LoRe: Dual Queries with Low Rank Approximation Re-ranking for In-Context Learning

definitions.text: The ability of a model to rapidly adapt to new downstream tasks beyond its initial training set, indicating how well it builds on existing knowledge for future learning.
definitions.paper: Large Language Models as Generalizable Policies for Embodied Tasks

definitions.text: The model's capacity to handle diverse tasks using the same architecture without requiring specialized, task-specific designs.
definitions.paper: GITA: Graph to Visual and Textual Integration for Vision-Language Graph Reasoning

definitions.text: The extent to which an optimizer uses parameter-specific or layer-specific preconditioning rather than a single global update rule.
definitions.paper: Deconstructing What Makes a Good Optimizer for Autoregressive Language Models

definitions.text: The ability of a large language model to evaluate and modify its own behavior in real-time in response to the specific demands of a given task or prompt, without external intervention.
definitions.paper: Transformer-Squared: Self-adaptive LLMs

definitions.text: The latent capacity of a language model to flexibly adjust its behavior to new tasks or scenarios without retraining.
definitions.paper: ELICIT: LLM Augmentation Via External In-context Capability

definitions.text: The model's ability to maintain performance when evaluated on data from a different domain or distribution than its training data.
definitions.paper: Youku Dense Caption: A Large-scale Chinese Video Dense Caption Dataset and Benchmarks

definitions.text: The capacity to reuse representations learned from speech and music for bioacoustic tasks.
definitions.paper: NatureLM-audio: an Audio-Language Foundation Model for Bioacoustics

definitions.text: The model's ability to adjust its output distribution to better suit a specific domain, such as medicine or law, without undergoing additional training.
definitions.paper: Chunk-Distilled Language Modeling

definitions.text: The extent to which learned table representations transfer across different tables and schemas rather than overfitting to a specific table or prompt context.
definitions.paper: Bridging the Semantic Gap Between Text and Table: A Case Study on NL2SQL

definitions.text: The degree to which a model's performance transfers across different operating systems and environments (web, desktop, mobile) without specific training on all platforms.
definitions.paper: Navigating the Digital World as Humans Do: Universal Visual Grounding for GUI Agents

definitions.text: The latent capacity of a model to adjust its behavior to meet the requirements of a particular downstream task through parameter modification.
definitions.paper: Text-to-LoRA: Instant Transformer Adaption

definitions.text: The latent ability of the model to effectively learn and perform on new tasks in a sequential setting without access to prior data.
definitions.paper: Dynamic Mixture of Curriculum LoRA Experts for Continual Multimodal Instruction Tuning

definitions.text: The ability to shift behavior when rules or contingencies change, rather than persisting with a previously successful strategy.
definitions.paper: Reflection-Bench: Evaluating Epistemic Agency in Large Language Models

definitions.text: The latent ability to adapt to complex, non-Gaussian, and multimodal posterior shapes without being constrained by parametric variational families.
definitions.paper: Can Transformers Learn Full Bayesian Inference in Context?

definitions.text: The ability to dynamically modify behavior in response to real-time user corrections, constraints, or changes in task goals during execution.
definitions.paper: Hi Robot: Open-Ended Instruction Following with Hierarchical Vision-Language-Action Models

definitions.text: The capacity of a model to adjust its internal representations or reasoning based on the specific task context, such as class definitions or labeling goals, without fine-tuning.
definitions.paper: Generalization Principles for Inference over Text-Attributed Graphs with Large Language Models

definitions.text: The latent ability of a multi-agent system to dynamically adjust its communication topology complexity based on the difficulty and nature of the given task, optimizing efficiency and performance.
definitions.paper: G-Designer: Architecting Multi-agent Communication Topologies via Graph Neural Networks

definitions.text: The latent ability of an LLM to adjust to specialized subject-matter content in vertical domains such as medicine, agriculture, geography, and finance.
definitions.paper: Test-Time Learning for Large Language Models

definitions.text: The capacity of a speculative decoding method to quickly adjust its draft generation to new or low-resource domains without retraining, by leveraging generalizable patterns from cached n-grams.
definitions.paper: HICode: Hierarchical Inductive Coding withLLMs

characterization: Across the surveyed literature, "Adaptability" is a broad construct, often used interchangeably with terms like "domain adaptation" and "flexibility," that predominantly refers to a model's capacity to adjust its behavior and performance to new tasks, domains, or data distributions without requiring full retraining. The prevailing usage frames this as aligning a model with specific downstream tasks based on their distributional characteristics or maintaining performance when faced with a domain shift. A smaller subset of work defines adaptability more specifically, for instance as "self-adaptation," where a model modifies its own behavior in real-time without external intervention, or as "adaptivity," which refers to an optimizer's use of parameter-specific update rules. The literature also contains discussion on whether adaptation represents true new learning or, as one paper asks, if it "simply draw[s] out capabilities that the model had already learned" (A Benchmark for Learning to Translate a New Language from One Grammar Book). The construct is operationalized and measured through model performance on a wide array of benchmarks. Papers in this set use instruments such as AndroidWorld and OSWORLD to evaluate adaptability in dynamic agent tasks, while others like Mind2Web, ScreenSpot, AG News, and ChemProt are used to assess performance on new tasks and domains. Adaptability is frequently studied alongside and considered related to Generalization, and some work reports that planning and compositionality can influence a model's adaptive capabilities.
